title: Reading and Use of English - Part 6
description: You are going to read four extracts from internet articles about technology in sports. For questions 37 – 40, choose from the experts A – D. The expert may be chosen more than once.

content.title: Back from the Dead: The Ethics of De-extinction

texts.A: The prospect of bringing back the woolly mammoth or the passenger pigeon is often presented as a triumph of genetic engineering. [37]However, I believe this is a dangerous diversion of resources; every dollar spent on 'resurrecting' a single extinct species is a dollar not spent on protecting the thousands of living species currently on the brink of collapse.[/37] [39]Furthermore, an animal is more than its DNA; it is a product of its environment and social structures, both of which are now gone.[/39] We would be creating biological curiosities, not restoring ecosystems.
texts.B: De-extinction is not about vanity; it is about ecological restoration. Large herbivores like the mammoth played a crucial role in maintaining the grasslands of the Arctic. [38]I disagree with the view that this technology is a threat to current conservation; on the contrary, the advancements made in cloning and synthetic biology will provide new tools to save species that are struggling today.[/38] [40]That said, we must be careful not to view this as a 'get out of jail free' card for our past environmental sins.[/40] Resurrecting one species does not justify the destruction of another.
texts.C: The moral imperative to pursue de-extinction is clear: if we caused the extinction, we have a duty to reverse it. [37]I share the view that this field could revolutionize conservation, creating a 'bio-bank' that acts as an insurance policy for the planet's biodiversity.[/37] [39]I am less convinced than some, however, that these animals would be 'out of place'; many ecosystems have remained relatively stable and could easily re-integrate their former inhabitants.[/39] We should embrace the role of 'gardeners' of the Earth, using our tech to heal the scars we've left behind.
texts.D: The hype around de-extinction ignores the potential for catastrophic 'eco-shocks.' Introducing a resuscitated species into a modern ecosystem is essentially introducing an invasive species from the past. [38]I share the concern that we are prioritizing 'charismatic megafauna' over the less glamorous work of habitat protection.[/38] [40]Unlike those who believe we can control the outcome, I fear that we are playing with a complex system we don't fully understand.[/40] The ethics of creating a sentient being just to satisfy our scientific curiosity are deeply questionable. We should focus on the future, not a romanticized version of the past.

questions.question: Which expert has a different view from C on the impact of de-extinction research on general conservation efforts?
questions.explanation: C believes de-extinction is a 'revolution' and an 'insurance policy' for biodiversity. A, however, sees it as a 'dangerous diversion of resources' that takes money away from protecting living species.

questions.question: Which expert expresses a similar opinion to A regarding the prioritization of de-extinction over other conservation work?
questions.explanation: A argues that resources are being diverted from 'living species.' D shares this concern, stating that we are 'prioritizing charismatic megafauna' over the 'less glamorous work of habitat protection.'

questions.question: Which expert shares C's perspective on whether resurrected animals could successfully live in today's world?
questions.explanation: C believes ecosystems are 'stable' and could 'easily re-integrate' species. B shares this logic by arguing that resuscitating large herbivores is 'about ecological restoration' of specific areas like the Arctic.

questions.question: Which expert has a different view from B on our ability to predict the consequences of reintroducing extinct species?
questions.explanation: B presents de-extinction as a tool for 'restoration' with specific ecological goals. D disagrees, fearing we are 'playing with a complex system we don't fully understand' and risking 'catastrophic eco-shocks.'
